title: The Day of Judgment
subtitle: Resurrection, the Scale, and the Books of Deeds in the Quran and the Bible
summary: Islam and Christianity both teach that history ends in a bodily resurrection and a final judgment before God, where every soul's deeds are examined and nothing is hidden. The Quran pictures this Day of Resurrection through a scale weighing even an atom's weight of good or evil and a record of deeds placed in each person's hand. The Bible pictures Jesus separating the nations like a shepherd separating sheep from goats, with books of deeds opened before God's throne. This article compares both pictures in detail, showing what the two faiths share and where they differ over who judges and how mercy is granted.

seo.metaTitle: The Day of Judgment: Quran and Bible Compared
seo.metaDescription: How the Quran's scale and record of deeds and the Bible's sheep-and-goats judgment describe the resurrection and final reckoning, compared for beginners.

sections.title: In Plain Words
sections.body:
Islam and Christianity agree on something enormous: this life is not the end of the story. Both faiths teach that a day is coming when every person who has ever lived will be raised from death, stand before God, and answer for how they lived.

The Quran calls this event the Day of Resurrection, or simply "That Day." It pictures a moment when even the smallest deed, as small as a single atom, is weighed on a scale of perfect justice and recorded in a book each person receives in their own hand. The New Testament pictures Jesus returning in glory to gather all nations before him, separating people the way a shepherd separates sheep from goats, while books recording every deed are opened before God's throne.

This article compares what each scripture actually says: the scattering of deeds and the scale of justice in the Quran, and the sheep and goats, the opened books, and the judgment seat of Christ in the New Testament. It closes by asking why Islam frames that day as both perfectly just and directly merciful, without needing anyone else to stand in a person's place.

sections.title: Introduction: A Day Neither Faith Lets You Forget
sections.body:
A shared conviction runs through mosque sermons and church sermons alike: what you do in this life matters forever, because a day of reckoning is coming. In English this is called the Day of Judgment, and it is one of the few doctrines where Islam and Christianity stand closer to each other than either does to many other worldviews.

Both scriptures reject two comforting but very different ideas: that death simply ends everything, and that a person may do as they wish because no final account will ever be settled. Instead, both describe a specific future event: the resurrection of the dead, followed by a judgment before God in which nothing is hidden and nothing is forgotten.

For someone new to either faith, this doctrine often lands as the most serious part of the religion. It turns everyday choices - honesty, generosity, cruelty, neglect - into matters of eternal consequence.

sections.title: The Question This Article Answers
sections.body:
The question at the center of this article is: how does the Quran describe the Day of Judgment, how does the Bible describe it, and what do the similarities and differences reveal about how each faith understands justice, mercy, and mediation between the sinner and God?

Both scriptures agree on the basic architecture of the event: a bodily resurrection, a gathering of all humanity before God, an examination of deeds symbolized by scales in the Quran and opened books in the Bible, and a permanent separation into two final destinies.

Where the two traditions differ is in the mechanics of the verdict: what exactly is weighed, who stands between the sinner and God, and how forgiveness reaches an imperfect human being. The Quran presents direct, personal accountability before God alone. The Bible presents Jesus Christ seated on the throne of judgment, with much of historic Christian theology holding that a believer's standing before that throne depends on their relationship to Christ as well as their deeds.

sections.title: Resurrection and Reckoning Before and After Muhammad
sections.body:
Belief in bodily resurrection and a final moral reckoning was not universal in the ancient world. Many ancient societies imagined the dead continuing in a shadowy existence, with little connection between how a person lived and what happened afterward. Against that backdrop, both the biblical and Quranic pictures of a coming Day of Judgment stand out as a far more morally charged vision of the afterlife.

The New Testament assumes bodily resurrection and final judgment as settled convictions. The book of Acts records that this had already become a live debate among Jewish groups in the first century, some affirming a future resurrection and some denying it. The Gospels and letters of the New Testament consistently teach that Jesus himself will return to judge the living and the dead.

The Quran was revealed in seventh-century Arabia, where many of the Prophet Muhammad's pagan contemporaries in Mecca found bodily resurrection almost absurd, doubting that decayed bones could ever be raised again. Because of that skepticism, many of the Quran's earliest revealed chapters, including the short Surah al-Zalzalah quoted below, insist on the reality, certainty, and exact fairness of the coming Day. For early Muslims, as for early Christians, the Day of Judgment was never a peripheral idea.

sections.title: The Day of Judgment in the Quran
sections.body:
The Quran's short ninety-ninth chapter, Surah al-Zalzalah, "The Earthquake," opens with the world convulsing at the end of time, the earth shaking and giving up what is inside it. Out of that upheaval comes a description of what happens to human beings:

يَوْمَئِذٍۢ يَصْدُرُ ٱلنَّاسُ أَشْتَاتًۭا لِّيُرَوْا۟ أَعْمَٰلَهُمْ

"That Day, the people will depart separated [into categories] to be shown [the result of] their deeds." (Quran 99:6)

People do not face judgment as an undifferentiated crowd; they leave already sorted, according to their deeds, into distinct outcomes. The next two verses make the standard of that sorting startlingly precise:

فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًۭا يَرَهُۥ

"So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it," (Quran 99:7)

وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍۢ شَرًّۭا يَرَهُۥ

"And whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it." (Quran 99:8)

Nothing is too small to count: not a private kindness no one else noticed, and not a small cruelty a person hoped had been forgotten.

This precision is not left abstract; the Quran gives it a concrete image, the scale (mizan). In Surah al-Anbiya, "The Prophets," God says:

وَنَضَعُ ٱلْمَوَٰزِينَ ٱلْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ فَلَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌۭ شَيْـًۭٔا ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍۢ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنَا بِهَا ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِنَا حَٰسِبِينَ

"And We place the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection, so no soul will be treated unjustly at all. And if there is [even] the weight of a mustard seed, We will bring it forth. And sufficient are We as accountant." (Quran 21:47)

The mustard seed, like the atom of the earlier verses, says no unit of good or evil is too small to register. The verse names God directly as the Accountant, needing no witness to correct His count.

Alongside the scale, the Quran describes a written record (kitab) handed to each person individually. Surah al-Inshiqaq, "The Splitting Open," describes the reception of that record:

فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ

"Then as for he who is given his record in his right hand," (Quran 84:7)

فَسَوْفَ يُحَاسَبُ حِسَابًۭا يَسِيرًۭا

"He will be judged with an easy account" (Quran 84:8)

Receiving the record in the right hand is a sign, throughout the Quran, of a life that met God's approval, and the reckoning that follows is gentle rather than crushing. The same passage describes a different reception for those whose record testifies against them. Together, the scale and the record give Quranic eschatology its shape: an individual, exact accounting before a Judge who needs no one else to establish the truth.

The record in the right hand shows that the accounting is individual; a separate verse states just as directly that the guilt behind a bad record cannot be shifted onto someone else. Surah al-Isra, "The Night Journey," says:

مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًۭا

"Whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul. And whoever errs only errs against it. And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And never would We punish until We sent a messenger." (Quran 17:15)

This, rather than the description of the record in the right hand, is the Quran's explicit statement that one soul's guilt is never transferred to or borne by another - a claim the conclusion below returns to directly.

sections.title: The Day of Judgment in the Bible
sections.body:
The New Testament's clearest single picture of the final judgment comes in Jesus's own words in the Gospel of Matthew. Describing his own return, Jesus says:

"But when the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the holy angels with him, then he will sit on the throne of his glory.

Before him all the nations will be gathered, and he will separate them one from another, as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats." (Matthew 25:31-32)

In the parable that follows, the sheep and goats are separated according to how they treated the hungry, the thirsty, the stranger, and the imprisoned - a standard of practical mercy many readers, Christian and Muslim alike, recognize as close to the Quran's own commands to feed the poor and care for the orphan.

The book of Revelation describes the same event from a different angle, using courtroom imagery rather than a pasture:

"I saw the dead, the great and the small, standing before the throne, and they opened books. Another book was opened, which is the book of life. The dead were judged out of the things which were written in the books, according to their works." (Revelation 20:12)

Here the record-keeping motif of the Quran's scale and written record reappears in similar form: books opened before the divine throne, containing the deeds of "the great and the small" alike, judged "according to their works." A further book, the book of life, is named alongside the books of deeds, tying the verdict to belonging as well as behavior.

The apostle Paul reinforces the same expectation in two of his letters. To the Corinthians he writes:

"For we must all be revealed before the judgment seat of Christ; that each one may receive the things in the body, according to what he has done, whether good or bad." (2 Corinthians 5:10)

And to the Romans, warning believers against judging one another prematurely, he states the same principle even more simply:

"So then each one of us will give account of himself to God." (Romans 14:12)

Together, these passages describe a final judgment that is universal, based on deeds, and presided over by Christ, named as the one seated on the throne of judgment. Mainstream Christian theology holds that Jesus is not only the coming Judge but, in this life, the believer's advocate and the ground of their acquittal, so the verdict a Christian expects is shaped by their relationship to Christ as much as by an independent tally of deeds. Christian traditions differ on how faith and works relate to that verdict, but all agree no one is exempt from standing before that throne.

sections.title: What the Two Pictures Share, and Where They Part
sections.body:
Set the two pictures side by side, and the agreement is striking before the disagreement even comes into view. Both scriptures teach a literal, bodily resurrection - not a vague survival of the soul, but people standing before God. Both teach a judgment that is total and universal: the Quran's "whoever does an atom's weight" (Quran 99:7-8) and Revelation's "the dead, the great and the small" (Revelation 20:12) refuse to let anyone imagine themselves too insignificant, or too important, to be judged. Both use the image of a written record: the Quran's book placed in the hand (Quran 84:7), and Revelation's books opened before the throne (Revelation 20:12). Both insist that deeds form part of the evidence: "according to what he has done" (2 Corinthians 5:10) parallels "to be shown [the result of] their deeds" (Quran 99:6) almost precisely. And both name a moment of separation into two final groups, whether Matthew's sheep and goats (Matthew 25:32) or the Quran's people "departing separated [into categories]" (Quran 99:6).

The difference that matters most is the identity of the judge. In the Quran, God alone judges and keeps account, described directly as "sufficient... as accountant" (Quran 21:47), with no figure standing between the individual and God as the ground of acquittal. The Quran itself makes this conditional character of intercession explicit; in Ayat al-Kursi, God asks, "Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (Quran 2:255). Any intercession granted to the Prophet is therefore not an independent ground of acquittal but something that operates only within limits God sets, and it does not change the deeds actually weighed. In the New Testament, Jesus Christ is seated on "the throne of his glory" (Matthew 25:31) and before his "judgment seat" all must appear (2 Corinthians 5:10); Christ is understood not only as judge but as the means by which a believer's sins are covered, a role Islam does not accept, since it holds each soul answers directly for itself before God.

A second difference is emphasis: the Quran's scale gives Islamic eschatology an almost mathematical precision, while the biblical picture leans more on shepherding imagery.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
First: "Islam teaches that a person earns Paradise purely by good deeds, with no room for mercy." This is a mistaken caricature. Sahih Muslim 2816c records the Prophet Muhammad teaching that no one's deeds alone entitle that person to Paradise, not even the Prophet, except through God's mercy; the weighing of deeds (Quran 21:47, 99:7-8) establishes perfect justice, but entry into Paradise depends on mercy responding to faith and sincere effort, not deeds as a purchase price.

Second: "Christianity teaches that a believer's deeds do not matter at the final judgment, since salvation is by faith alone." This oversimplifies. The passages quoted above - "each one will give account of himself to God" (Romans 14:12), judgment "according to what he has done, whether good or bad" (2 Corinthians 5:10), and "judged... according to their works" (Revelation 20:12) - show that mainstream Christian teaching holds every believer accountable for their deeds. Theologians differ on how that fits with justification by faith, but none deny that works are examined.

Third: "The Quran only cares about deeds, and the Bible only cares about who Christ is." Both foreground deeds heavily - the Quran's atom's weight of good or evil (Quran 99:7-8) and the Bible's books "according to their works" (Revelation 20:12). The real dividing line is whether a mediating figure stands between the sinner and the verdict, not whether deeds matter.

Fourth, some assume the sheep-and-goats parable is only about correct doctrine. In the fuller parable, the criterion Jesus names is mercy shown to the hungry, the thirsty, the stranger, and the imprisoned - close to the Quran's own commands to feed the poor and care for the orphan and the traveler.

sections.title: Conclusion: A Just and Merciful Reckoning, With No One Bearing Another's Burden
sections.body:
From an Islamic point of view, the Quran's picture of the Day of Judgment answers a question every serious person eventually asks: if life so often feels unfair, is there a court where the account will finally, exactly be set right? The Quran's answer is yes, and it is emphatic about the precision of that court. Nothing is too small to matter - not an atom's weight of good, not an atom's weight of evil (Quran 99:7-8) - and no soul will be wronged even by the weight of a mustard seed (Quran 21:47).

Muslims find something theologically satisfying in the directness of this picture. Each person stands before God carrying their own record (Quran 84:7), and the Quran states the deeper principle behind that record explicitly: "no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another" (Quran 17:15). No one else's righteousness covers their failures, and no one else's guilt is punished in their place. God alone is sufficient as the Accountant (Quran 21:47) - not because Islam denies that mercy is real, but because it holds that mercy does not require an intermediary's suffering to be activated. A repentant sinner is forgiven because God chooses to forgive, directly, the same God who set the scale.

This is where the Islamic case is made most confidently: a Day of Judgment in which perfect justice and perfect mercy sit together without needing a mediator who bears a penalty meant for someone else. Christians rightly cherish their own scripture's insistence that Christ will judge the nations and that every person will give account of themselves. Islam asks readers to consider whether that same seriousness about accountability points most naturally toward a Judge who deals with each soul directly, without needing anyone else to stand in the accused's place.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Do Muslims and Christians actually agree that a Day of Judgment is coming?
A: Yes. Both scriptures teach a bodily resurrection of the dead, a universal judgment before God, and a final separation between two ultimate destinies.

Q: What is the "scale" (mizan) mentioned in the Quran?
A: The mizan is the Quran's image of a scale of justice on which every deed is weighed. Quran 21:47 says God places these "scales of justice" so precisely that "no soul will be treated unjustly at all," down to a mustard seed.

Q: Does the Bible teach that people are judged by their good and bad deeds?
A: Yes. Revelation 20:12, 2 Corinthians 5:10, and Romans 14:12 all describe judgment based on a person's actual deeds - "according to what he has done, whether good or bad" - not merely stated beliefs.

Q: Who acts as judge on the Day of Judgment in each faith?
A: In the Quran, God alone judges, sufficient "as accountant" (Quran 21:47). In the New Testament, Jesus Christ returns "in his glory" to judge all nations (Matthew 25:31-32), and before his "judgment seat" everyone must appear (2 Corinthians 5:10).

Q: What is the "book" or "record" of deeds?
A: Both scriptures use a written record of a person's life. The Quran describes a record given into the right or left hand (Quran 84:7); Revelation 20:12 describes books opened before God's throne, alongside a separate "book of life."

Q: Does Islam believe in intercession on the Day of Judgment?
A: Many Islamic scholars affirm that intercession (shafa'ah) by the Prophet Muhammad on behalf of believers will occur, but the Quran ties any such intercession strictly to God's own permission - "Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (Quran 2:255) - so it is read as an added mercy, not a substitute payment that changes the deeds weighed on the scale.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The following works are offered as further reading, listed for orientation rather than as sources for specific claims in this article.

Jane I. Smith and Yvonne Y. Haddad — The Islamic Understanding of Death and Resurrection: a study of Islamic eschatology drawing on the Quran, hadith, and classical commentary.

Seyyed Hossein Nasr (general editor) — The Study Quran: A New Translation and Commentary: an English translation with commentary on the Quran's passages about resurrection and judgment.

N.T. Wright — Surprised by Hope: a widely read study of the Christian doctrine of resurrection and final judgment.

Jerry L. Walls (editor) — The Oxford Handbook of Eschatology: a scholarly survey of afterlife beliefs across religious traditions, including Islam and Christianity.

John L. Esposito — Islam: The Straight Path: a standard introductory overview of Islamic belief and practice.

quranVerses.translation: And whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: And We place the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection, so no soul will be treated unjustly at all. And if there is [even] the weight of a mustard seed, We will bring it forth. And sufficient are We as accountant.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: Then as for he who is given his record in his right hand,
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: He will be judged with an easy account
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًۭا
quranVerses.translation: Whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul. And whoever errs only errs against it. And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And never would We punish until We sent a messenger.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌۭ وَلَا نَوْمٌۭ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍۢ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ
quranVerses.translation: Allah - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of [all] existence. Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them, and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High, the Most Great.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text:
“But when the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the holy angels with him, then he will sit on the throne of his glory.

Before him all the nations will be gathered, and he will separate them one from another, as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats.

I saw the dead, the great and the small, standing before the throne, and they opened books. Another book was opened, which is the book of life. The dead were judged out of the things which were written in the books, according to their works.
For we must all be revealed before the judgment seat of Christ; that each one may receive the things in the body, according to what he has done, whether good or bad.
So then each one of us will give account of himself to God.
Before him all the nations will be gathered, and he will separate them one from another, as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats.
“But when the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the holy angels with him, then he will sit on the throne of his glory.

faq.question: Do Muslims and Christians actually agree that a Day of Judgment is coming?
faq.answer: Yes. Both scriptures teach a bodily resurrection of the dead, a universal judgment before God, and a final separation between two ultimate destinies.

faq.question: What is the "scale" (mizan) mentioned in the Quran?
faq.answer: The mizan is the Quran's image of a scale of justice on which every deed is weighed. Quran 21:47 says God places these "scales of justice" so precisely that "no soul will be treated unjustly at all," down to a mustard seed.

faq.answer: Yes. Revelation 20:12, 2 Corinthians 5:10, and Romans 14:12 all describe judgment based on a person's actual deeds - "according to what he has done, whether good or bad" - not merely stated beliefs.

faq.answer: Both scriptures use a written record of a person's life. The Quran describes a record given into the right or left hand (Quran 84:7); Revelation 20:12 describes books opened before God's throne, alongside a separate "book of life."

faq.answer: Many Islamic scholars affirm that intercession (shafa'ah) by the Prophet Muhammad on behalf of believers will occur, but the Quran ties any such intercession strictly to God's own permission - "Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (Quran 2:255) - so it is read as an added mercy, not a substitute payment that changes the deeds weighed on the scale.
